eli3: Expectation (mean) is the long-run average value: E[X] = sum of (value × probability). For a fair die: E[X] = (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 3.5. It's the 'center of mass' of the distribution.

Variance measures spread: Var(X) = E[(X - E[X])²] = average squared distance from mean. Standard deviation is √Var — same units as original variable. Small variance = concentrated; large variance = spread out.

Linearity of expectation is powerful: E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y], always (even if X and Y dependent). But Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) only if X, Y uncorrelated. Covariance measures how X and Y vary together.

whenWhy.title: Loss functions and risk minimization
whenWhy.body: MSE = E[(Y - Ŷ)²] is expected squared error. MAE = E[|Y - Ŷ|]. Expected loss drives decision theory. Bayes risk is minimum expected loss over decisions. Value at Risk (VaR) is quantile of expected loss.

whenWhy.title: Portfolio theory and risk
whenWhy.body: Expected return = weighted sum of asset returns. Portfolio variance depends on covariances: σₚ² = wᵀΣw. Diversification reduces variance when assets aren't perfectly correlated. Mean-variance optimization balances return vs risk.

whenWhy.title: Bias-Variance tradeoff
whenWhy.body: MSE = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Error. Understanding expectation and variance decomposition is crucial for model selection, regularization, and ensemble methods.

whenWhy.title: A/B testing and experimental design
whenWhy.body: Compare E[Y|Treatment] vs E[Y|Control]. Standard error of mean estimate depends on Var(Y)/n. Power calculations require specifying expected effect size and variance.

deepDiveIntro: For discrete X: E[X] = Σ x P(X=x). For continuous X: E[X] = ∫ x f(x)dx. Expectation exists if E[|X|] < ∞. Linearity: E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] (no independence needed). For any function g: E[g(X)] = Σ g(x)P(X=x) or ∫ g(x)f(x)dx (law of the unconscious statistician).

Variance: Var(X) = E[(X-μ)²] = E[X²] - μ². Standard deviation σ = √Var(X). Covariance: Cov(X,Y) = E[(X-μₓ)(Y-μᵧ)] = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]. Correlation: ρ = Cov(X,Y)/(σₓσᵧ) ∈ [-1,1]. If X ⟂ Y, Cov(X,Y) = 0 (converse false: uncorrelated ≠ independent).

deepDive.heading: Expectation (Mean)
deepDive.body: Discrete: E[X] = Σ xᵢ p(xᵢ). Continuous: E[X] = ∫ x f(x)dx. Expectation is linear: E[Σ aᵢXᵢ] = Σ aᵢE[Xᵢ]. For any function g: E[g(X)] uses LOTUS (law of unconscious statistician) — no need to find distribution of g(X).
deepDive.formula: E[X] = Σ x p(x) or ∫ x f(x)dx; E[g(X)] = Σ g(x)p(x) or ∫ g(x)f(x)dx

deepDive.heading: Variance and Standard Deviation
deepDive.body: Var(X) = E[(X-μ)²] = E[X²] - μ². SD(X) = √Var(X). Variance scales quadratically: Var(aX) = a²Var(X). For independent X,Y: Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y). Standard deviation of sample mean: σ/√n (standard error).

deepDive.heading: Covariance and Correlation
deepDive.body: Cov(X,Y) = E[(X-μₓ)(Y-μᵧ)] = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]. Positive: tend to be on same side of means. Negative: opposite sides. Correlation ρ = Cov(X,Y)/(σₓσᵧ) ∈ [-1,1]. ρ = ±1 iff Y = aX + b (linear relationship).

deepDive.heading: Moments and Moment Generating Functions
deepDive.body: kth moment: E[Xᵏ]. kth central moment: E[(X-μ)ᵏ]. Skewness (3rd standardized moment): asymmetry. Kurtosis (4th): tail heaviness. MGF M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]; if exists near 0, uniquely determines distribution. M_X⁽ᵏ⁾(0) = E[Xᵏ].

deepDive.heading: Conditional Expectation
deepDive.body: E[Y|X=x] is expected Y given X=x. Function of x: regression function. Law of total expectation: E[Y] = E[E[Y|X]]. Law of total variance: Var(Y) = E[Var(Y|X)] + Var(E[Y|X]) (explained + unexplained variance).

takeaways: E[X] = Σ x p(x) or ∫ x f(x)dx. Long-run average, center of mass.
Var(X) = E[(X-μ)²] = E[X²] - μ². Spread, quadratically scaled.
Linearity of expectation always holds. Var(X+Y) needs independence (or correction for covariance).
Conditional expectation E[Y|X] is the regression function.

edgeCases.a: If E[|X|] = ∞, expectation is undefined. Example: Cauchy distribution (t with 1 df) has no mean — it's symmetric with heavy tails. Sample means of Cauchy don't converge (violate LLN). Stable distributions with α < 1 have no mean.
Yes. Example: X ~ Uniform(-1,1), Y = X². Cov(X,Y) = E[X³] - E[X]E[X²] = 0 - 0 = 0 (uncorrelated). But Y is completely determined by X (dependent). Correlation only captures linear relationships.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between variance and standard deviation?
edgeCases.a: Variance is in squared units (harder to interpret). SD is in original units (e.g., if X is in dollars, SD is in dollars, variance in dollars²). SD is used for confidence intervals and describing spread. Variance is mathematically nicer (adds for independent variables, quadratic form).

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
